text:
# Abstract

The rapid evolution of generative pre-trained transformer (GPT) models has raised significant concerns regarding the misuse of AI-generated content in academic settings, sparking a race to develop effective detection strategies. This study evaluates the efficacy of these detection tools, focusing on their accuracy and the nature of their classification errors. We specifically investigate whether current software can reliably distinguish between human-authored and ChatGPT-generated text, and whether common modifications—such as machine translation and content obfuscation—undermine their performance. 

Our research tests 14 tools, including 12 publicly available detectors and two widely used commercial systems, Turnitin and PlagiarismCheck. The results indicate that current detection technology is neither accurate nor reliable. Most tools exhibit a significant "human-writer bias," frequently misclassifying AI-generated output as human-authored. Furthermore, the effectiveness of these tools drops sharply when faced with content obfuscation techniques. 

This paper provides several key contributions: (1) a comprehensive summary of current scientific and non-scientific efforts in AI detection; (2) one of the most rigorous and extensive comparative tests to date, utilizing an original, controlled document set; and (3) a critical discussion of the practical and ethical limitations of using these tools to police academic integrity. We conclude that current detectors lack the robustness necessary for high-stakes academic decision-making.
